public long waysToBuyPensPencils(int total, int cost1, int cost2) {

    long totalWays = 0;

    int maxPens = total / cost1;

    for (int i = 0; i <= maxPens; i++) {

        int spentOnPens = i * cost1;

        int remaining = total - spentOnPens;

        int maxPencils = remaining / cost2;

        totalWays += (maxPencils + 1L);

    }

    return totalWays;

}

Wait, but what if cost2 is zero? Well, constraints say no.

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public long waysToBuyPensPencils(int total, int cost1, int cost2) {
        long totalWays = 0;
        int maxPens = total / cost1;
        for (int i = 0; i <= maxPens; i++) {
            int remaining = total - (i * cost1);
            int maxPencils = remaining / cost2;
            totalWays += (long) (maxPencils + 1);
        }
        return totalWays;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(total / cost1)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution iterates through all possible numbers of pens (0 to max possible) and calculates the remaining money. For each case, it computes the maximum number of pencils that can be bought and adds the count of valid combinations. Key strengths include simplicity and linearity, while the potential weakness is linear time in worst-case scenarios.
